title: Cabbage rolls
subtitle: Blanched cabbage leaves wrapped around a pork-and-beef and rice filling, braised in tomato.
excerpt: The slow-Sunday dish of central and eastern Europe. Soft savoy cabbage leaves are wrapped around a filling of pork, beef, rice, and dill, packed snug in a heavy pan, and braised slowly in a light tomato sauce until the leaves are silky and the filling has taken on the flavour of the broth.
sourceNotes: Stuffed cabbage rolls go by a different name in every country across the eastern European belt: golabki in Polish, holubky in Slovak, sarma in Croatian and Serbian, golubtsi in Russian and Ukrainian, töltött káposzta in Hungarian. The dish is older than most of the names; cabbage leaves wrapping a grain-and-meat filling appear in regional cookery from at least the seventeenth century. The technique is one of frugality and patience: cheap cuts of meat, rice or barley as bulker, and the long slow braise that turns the cabbage into something silken. The Polish-American version (heavy on tomato) is the one most familiar in British and American homes.

content.text: A whole savoy cabbage, twelve large leaves carefully blanched, a filling of half beef and half pork mixed with cooked rice and chopped dill, rolled up and packed tight in a casserole. The pan goes into the oven for an hour and a half. The cabbage softens to silk; the filling holds together; the sauce reduces and concentrates. The rolls come out tender enough to lift with a fish slice.

Savoy cabbage has the best leaves for this: large, pliable, slightly crinkled so the sauce catches. White cabbage works if savoy is not available but the rolls are firmer. The leaves are blanched first to make them flexible enough to roll without cracking. Save the small inner leaves; chop them and line the bottom of the casserole. They keep the rolls from catching.

items.name: Dill
items.prepNote: finely chopped, fronds and tender stems

content.text: Cook the rice and soften the onion

Cook the {{long-grain-rice}} of rice in plenty of salted water for ten minutes, two minutes short of done. Drain and rinse with cold water; the rice will finish cooking inside the rolls. While the rice cooks, warm the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a small pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} chopped onion with a pinch of salt and cook for eight minutes, until soft. Let it cool.

Bring a stockpot of water to a boil. Cut a deep cone around the core of the {{cabbage-savoy}} with a small knife. Lower the whole cabbage into the boiling water for two minutes, then turn off the heat and use tongs to peel away the outer leaves as they soften. You need about twelve large outer leaves intact. Lift them onto a tea towel to drain. Pare down the thick rib at the base of each leaf with a paring knife so the leaf is uniformly thin and rolls without cracking.
Chop the small inner cabbage leaves coarsely and set aside for the casserole base.

Combine the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince, the {{pork-mince}} of pork mince, the cooked rice, the cooled onion, the {{garlic}} of grated garlic, the {{dill}} of chopped dill, the {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, the {{eggs}} egg, a generous pinch of salt, and a turn of pepper in a large bowl. Mix with your hands until just combined and uniform.

Lay a cabbage leaf flat on the board, rib-side down. Spoon two heaped tablespoons of filling near the stem end. Fold the stem end up over the filling, then fold the two sides in towards the centre, and roll firmly away from you to make a tight parcel. Repeat with the remaining leaves and filling; you should get twelve rolls. Cover the base of a heavy lidded casserole with the chopped inner cabbage leaves. Pack the rolls into the casserole in two layers, seam-side down, snug against each other so they cannot unfold.

Heat the oven to 160°C. Pour the {{tomato-passata}} of passata and the {{stock-beef}} of hot stock over the rolls. Tuck in the {{bay-leaves}} of bay. The liquid should come about three-quarters of the way up the rolls; top up with hot water if not. Cover and bring to a simmer on the hob, then transfer to the oven for one and a half hours. Check at the hour mark: the cabbage should be tender enough to cut with a fork; the sauce should have reduced and clung. Serve with a generous spoon of {{soured-cream}} of soured cream and a scatter of fresh dill.

items.fix: Return the leaf to the hot water for thirty seconds and try again. Pare the thick rib down to the same thickness as the leaf so the fold sits flat.
items.cause: Not blanched long enough, or the rib at the base of the leaf was too thick.

items.fix: Mix only until uniform; do not pack the meat. Stop the rice at ten minutes so the grain holds shape. Make sure the egg is in the mix.
items.cause: Mix was over-handled, rice over-cooked, or filling under-bound.
items.symptom: Filling is loose and falls out

items.fix: Place each roll seam-side down and pack snug against the next. The neighbours hold each other in place.
items.cause: Packed too loose, or seam-side up.

items.fix: Lift the lid for the last twenty minutes of the braise. The sauce reduces and concentrates.
items.cause: Too much liquid in the pot, or lid did not seal.
items.symptom: Sauce is thin and watery after braising

items.fix: Salt the filling generously when mixing. A test-fry of a teaspoon of filling tells you the seasoning before you commit.
items.cause: Under-seasoned, or salt only on the outside.
items.symptom: Filling is bland at the centre

content.text: Sauerkraut-juice in place of half the passata is the Slavic version (sarma). Smoked bacon chopped through the filling adds depth. For a vegetarian fill, swap the meat for cooked mushrooms, chopped walnuts, and a heavier hand of rice; use vegetable stock. The Hungarian töltött káposzta uses both fresh cabbage and a layer of sauerkraut underneath the rolls in the pot.

Cabbage rolls are the slow-Sunday dish of the eastern European kitchen, the one that takes the afternoon and feeds the family on Monday too. Polish kitchens make golabki, Slovak ones make holubky, Hungarian ones make töltött káposzta. The shape is shared across an enormous geography. Every country has stamped the same idea with a local name; every household has its preferred ratio of beef to pork.
